topic_summary: The Departing Australia Superannuation Payment (DASP) allows temporary residents who have left Australia and whose visa has ceased to claim their accumulated superannuation. Key decision points include visa type (which determines tax rates: non-WHM holders face 35% on the taxable component (taxed element) and 45% on the taxable component (untaxed element); WHM holders face 65% on both elements), super balance (balances of $5,000 or more trigger additional identification and certification requirements), timing (super funds transfer to the ATO as unclaimed money if 6 months or more have passed since departure), and whether super is held with a fund or the ATO. Payment is generally made within 28 days of a completed application being received, and the payer must issue a DASP payment summary within 14 days of making the payment.

questions.text: What best describes your current situation with Australia?

options.label: I visited Australia on a temporary visa, my visa has ceased, and I have already left Australia
I am still in Australia
I held a permanent Australian visa or am an Australian citizen
I am not sure about my visa type or residency status

questions.text: What is your main question about your Departing Australia Superannuation Payment (DASP)?

options.label: I want to confirm whether I am eligible to claim DASP
I want to know how much tax will be deducted from my DASP
I want to know what identification documents I need to apply
I want to know how long DASP takes to process and when I will be paid
I want to know what happens if I do not apply for DASP

questions.text: Did you hold a Working Holiday Maker (WHM) visa (subclass 417 or 462) at any point during your time working in Australia?

options.label: Yes, I held a Working Holiday Maker visa
No, I held a different type of temporary visa (e.g. student, skilled, sponsored)
I held both a WHM visa and another temporary visa at different times
I am not sure what visa subclass I held

questions.text: Is your super balance $5,000 or more?

options.label: Yes, my super balance is $5,000 or more
No, my super balance is below $5,000
I do not know my super balance

questions.text: Which aspect of the DASP timeline are you most interested in?

options.label: How long after I apply until I receive my payment (the 28-day processing window)
What happens if I wait too long — I am concerned about the 6-month threshold for transfer to the ATO
Both of the above apply to my situation
Neither of these — I have a different question

terminals.heading: Am I eligible to claim DASP? (Visa status and departure check)
terminals.result_copy: Review recommended: based on your answers, your situation shows signals consistent with DASP eligibility criteria (temporary visa, visa ceased, departed Australia). The dasp-eligibility-check dish covers the conditions that must be met and addresses edge cases such as former permanent residents whose status has lapsed.

terminals.heading: What tax will be deducted from my DASP? (Non-working holiday maker visa holders)
terminals.result_copy: Indicated risk of tax deduction: your profile indicates you did not hold a Working Holiday Maker visa. The applicable DASP tax rate indicated for the taxable component (taxed element) is 35%, and for the taxable component (untaxed element) is 45%. Review recommended before submitting your claim.

terminals.heading: What tax will be deducted from my DASP? (Working holiday maker visa holders)
terminals.result_copy: High-risk signal for tax deduction: your profile indicates you held a Working Holiday Maker visa. The applicable DASP tax rate indicated for the taxable component (taxed element) is 65%, and for the taxable component (untaxed element) is 65%. Review recommended before submitting your claim.

terminals.heading: What identification documents do I need for DASP if my super balance is $5,000 or more?
terminals.result_copy: Review recommended: your balance is at or above $5,000, which indicates your super fund may require certified copies of proof of identification documents. For paper applications to super funds, a Certification of Immigration Status from Home Affairs may also be required. Early action is indicated as these documents are easier to certify while still in Australia.

terminals.heading: What happens if I don't apply for DASP? (Super transferred to ATO after 6 months)
terminals.result_copy: Indicated risk: if 6 months or more have passed since you left Australia and your visa has ceased, your super fund is indicated to transfer your super to the ATO as unclaimed super money. Review recommended to determine whether this threshold applies to your situation.

terminals.heading: How long does DASP take to process and when will I receive my payment?
terminals.result_copy: Review recommended: your DASP will generally be paid within 28 days of your completed application being received. After payment, the payer is indicated to issue a DASP payment summary within 14 days of making the payment. These are the indicated timeframes; individual circumstances may vary.

terminals.heading: DASP may not apply to your situation
terminals.result_copy: Your answers suggest DASP may not fit — for example you are still in Australia, or you held permanent residence or citizenship, so the ordinary Australian super rules apply instead. A short personalised review works through what does apply to your circumstances and the steps to take next.

terminals.heading: Your situation needs a closer look
terminals.result_copy: Your answers do not yet confirm which DASP position applies to you — a key detail (such as your visa type, balance, or status) is unconfirmed. A short personalised review confirms where you stand and exactly what to check before you claim.

terminals.heading: A more complex situation
terminals.result_copy: Your answers point to more than one factor at play (for example mixed visa types, or more than one timeline concern), so a single clean answer does not apply. A short personalised review works through the interacting parts of your situation before you claim.
